agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing interacts with cancellation, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shipment, and delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled. Intended to be available before shipment per Product requirements."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Once created, it is considered irreversible by the Finance team."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Represents a point-of-no-return for the Product team's cancellation window."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."},{"term":"Pricing Lock-in","definition":"The business motivation for early invoicing — capturing the agreed price at time of placement to prevent price fluctuations."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product policy defining the cancellation window. It could be narrowed or expanded by business decision. However, it currently contradicts BR-2 and BR-3 in combination.","testable_assertion":"BLOCKED — cannot define a testable assertion until the contradiction between BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3 is resolved."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Reversing it would have accounting, audit, and potentially legal implications. This is a data/state integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, when a cancellation is attempted, then the system must reject the cancellation."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. A VP could change this to invoice at shipment or at a different lifecycle stage. The timing is a business choice, not a physical or integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, when placement is confirmed, then an invoice is immediately created and associated with the order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — under BR-3 the order is already invoiced at placement, so BR-2 blocks cancellation. But BR-1 says cancellation should be allowed since the order hasn't shipped. This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if the invoice doesn't exist yet, BR-1 would allow cancellation, but BR-3 says invoicing should have already happened. System behavior depends on which rule takes precedence."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if an order has multiple line items and some have shipped, the cancellation window per BR-1 is ambiguous. Not addressable until the primary contradiction is resolved."},{"scenario":"Credit note or reversal issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"Finance states invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes. If a credit note effectively neutralizes the invoice, cancellation might become possible — but this concept is absent from the requirements."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. If cancellation is truly impossible after placement (BR-2 + BR-3 win), customers who need to cancel will require manual refund processes, chargebacks, or credit notes — all of which are costly. If BR-1 wins, Finance must handle invoice reversals or voidances, complicating revenue recognition and audit trails.","operational":"High. The resolution determines the entire order lifecycle state machine. Every downstream process (warehouse picking, shipping, returns, refunds) depends on whether and when cancellation is permitted.","user":"Critical. Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If the system silently blocks all cancellations from the moment of placement, customer satisfaction and trust will erode significantly.","risk":"Regulatory and compliance risk if invoicing rules are violated. Customer churn risk if cancellation is blocked unexpectedly. Financial reconciliation risk if invoices are voided without proper accounting treatment.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite expectation that pre-shipment cancellation is possible. Will drive support tickets, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Product Team","impact":"Core feature requirement (cancel before ship) is unimplementable as stated. Needs to renegotiate with Finance/Ops."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation is allowed, they need a mechanism (credit notes, invoice voiding) that contradicts their stated irreversibility constraint."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is deferred to shipment instead of placement, they lose pricing lock-in. Need an alternative mechanism to lock pricing without invoicing."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of manual cancellation requests if the system blocks self-service cancellation."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Cannot implement until the contradiction is resolved. Any implementation choice will violate at least one stakeholder's requirements."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholders have provided requirements that form a logical contradiction. BR-2 + BR-3 together make BR-1 impossible: if every order is invoiced at placement and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, then no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM's requirement. No amount of analysis can resolve this without a business decision from the stakeholders. Confidence is below 50 due to the unresolvable contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a misreading — the three rules are logically incompatible","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is correctly classified as an invariant from a financial integrity standpoint","The stakeholder impact analysis is well-grounded"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which rule should yield — this is a business decision, not an analytical one","Whether the organization has an existing credit note or invoice voiding process that could resolve the tension","Whether 'invoiced' in Finance's language means the same thing as 'invoiced' in Operations' language"],"assumptions":["All three requirements are accurately stated and represent current policy","'Invoiced' means the same thing to both Finance and Operations","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal process that would soften BR-2","The order lifecycle is: Placement → (Invoicing) → Shipment → Delivery"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are logically incompatible. BR-3 states orders are invoiced at placement. BR-2 states invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Together, these mean no order can ever be cancelled after placement. This directly contradicts BR-1, which promises cancellation is available any time before shipment. At least one rule must yield.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Team","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Option A: Defer invoicing to shipment time (relaxes BR-3). Preserves cancellation window but Operations loses pricing lock-in at placement. Could introduce a separate 'price lock' mechanism that is not an invoice.","Option B: Allow invoice voiding or credit notes for cancelled orders (relaxes BR-2). Preserves both cancellation and early invoicing, but Finance must accept that invoicing is conditionally reversible.","Option C: Restrict cancellation to a very short grace period before invoicing completes (relaxes BR-1). Technically resolves the contradiction but may be so narrow as to be useless to customers.","Option D: Separate 'proforma invoice' from 'fiscal invoice' — issue a proforma at placement for pricing lock-in (revocable) and a fiscal invoice at shipment (irreversible). Cancellation is allowed before fiscal invoicing."],"recommendation":"Option D. Introduce a distinction between a proforma invoice (issued at placement for pricing lock-in, revocable) and a fiscal invoice (issued at or near shipment, irreversible). This preserves all three stakeholders' intent: customers can cancel before shipment, Finance's fiscal invoices remain irreversible, and Operations locks in pricing at placement. This is standard practice in logistics and e-commerce. However, this requires explicit agreement from all three stakeholders — I cannot unilaterally resolve this."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are logically contradictory and cannot be implemented faithfully.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is made impossible by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Implementing any two rules necessarily violates the third."}]}
